title: 10 - Blood Vessels - Exam Prep Questions
description: Third-party generated single-choice exam-prep questions for Chapter 10. The set is aligned primarily to the Chapter 10 textbook content and shaped to reflect the style, tone, and difficulty distribution of the RCPA Basic Pathological Sciences mock exam format. Questions prioritize the syllabus emphasis on arteriosclerosis, hypertension, atherosclerosis, aneurysms/dissection, and vasculitis.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These questions are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.answer_key_note: Answers are included as stable choice keys with brief source-grounded rationales for study and review purposes.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 10: Blood Vessels
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for topic emphasis and exam framing; vascular tumours and pathology of vascular intervention are not currently in syllabus emphasis.

source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used as a style, structure, and difficulty reference.

questions.question:
A 59-year-old man with atherosclerosis-related peripheral arterial disease is found to have endothelial activation at arterial branch points where flow is disturbed.

Which event BEST represents the earliest pathogenic step that permits atherosclerotic plaque formation?

choices.text: Endothelial dysfunction with increased permeability and leukocyte adhesion
Medial calcification of the internal elastic lamina
Smooth muscle apoptosis within an established plaque
Organization of a pre-existing mural thrombus

answer.reason: The response-to-injury model begins with endothelial dysfunction, which increases permeability, leukocyte adhesion, and thrombogenicity.

questions.question:
A patient with renovascular hypertension is treated with an ACE inhibitor.

Which effect MOST directly lowers the blood pressure?

answer.reason: ACE inhibitors lower blood pressure primarily by reducing angiotensin II formation, thereby decreasing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-driven sodium retention.

questions.question:
A kidney biopsy from a patient with malignant hypertension shows concentric laminated thickening of arteriolar walls with marked luminal narrowing.

What vascular lesion is MOST likely present?

answer.reason: Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis produces the classic onion-skin concentric laminated arteriolar thickening seen in severe or malignant hypertension.

questions.question:
A patient with long-standing benign hypertension and diabetes has homogeneous pink thickening of small arteriolar walls with luminal narrowing and chronic renal ischemic change.

What lesion BEST explains this morphology?

answer.reason: Hyaline arteriolosclerosis is the benign-hypertension pattern, also common in diabetes, and causes diffuse small-vessel narrowing and nephrosclerosis.

questions.question:
An elderly patient has calcified muscular arteries visible on imaging, but the vessel lumina remain patent and the lesion is considered largely incidental.

What is the BEST diagnosis?

answer.reason: Monckeberg medial sclerosis is medial calcification of muscular arteries, usually in older adults, and typically does not encroach on the lumen.

questions.question:
Six months after coronary stent placement, a patient develops restenosis at the treated segment.

What pathologic process MOST directly underlies this complication?

choices.text: Acute fibrinoid necrosis of the media

answer.reason: Restenosis after intervention reflects the stereotyped vascular healing response: SMC migration into the intima, proliferation, and ECM deposition.

questions.question: Which circulating lipoprotein is MOST protective against atherosclerosis because it mobilizes cholesterol from plaques and returns it to the liver?

answer.reason: HDL is protective because it removes cholesterol from developing and established plaques and transports it to the liver for excretion.

questions.question: Within an early atherosclerotic lesion, macrophages become foam cells primarily by ingesting which form of LDL?

choices.text: Acetylated LDL by the LDL receptor

answer.reason: Macrophages ingest oxidized LDL through scavenger receptors, producing foam cells within the intima.

questions.question: Minute extracellular cholesterol crystals in early atherosclerotic lesions act as danger signals that stimulate production of which mediator?

answer.reason: Cholesterol crystals activate innate immune pathways, including the inflammasome, leading to IL-1 production.

questions.question: In the coronary circulation, symptoms of stable angina typically appear when the lumen is narrowed by approximately what percentage?

answer.reason: In coronary arteries, demand usually exceeds supply and produces stable angina when stenosis reaches about 70%.

questions.question:
A patient suffers an acute myocardial infarction from a coronary lesion that had not previously caused significant exertional symptoms.

What is the MOST likely explanation?

choices.text: Chronic fixed stenosis gradually progressed to complete occlusion without thrombosis
Medial calcification abruptly obliterated the lumen
Plaque rupture or erosion triggered acute thrombosis on a vulnerable lesion
The lesion was a venous thrombus that embolized retrograde
Atherosclerotic plaques only cause infarction after years of stable angina

answer.reason: Many acute coronary syndromes arise when a previously asymptomatic vulnerable plaque ruptures or erodes and induces acute thrombosis.

questions.question:
A coronary plaque has a thin fibrous cap, a large lipid-rich necrotic core, and prominent inflammatory infiltrates.

How should this plaque be classified?

answer.reason: A thin cap, large lipid core, and dense inflammation are the defining features of a vulnerable plaque prone to rupture.

questions.question: In descending order of severity, which vascular bed is MOST commonly the most severely affected by atherosclerosis?

answer.reason: The infrarenal abdominal aorta is the site of greatest typical atherosclerotic severity, followed by coronary and selected peripheral/cerebral vessels.

questions.question: Which combination lists the major modifiable risk factors for atherosclerosis highlighted in Chapter 10?

choices.text: Age, male sex, family history, and hypertension
Clonal hematopoiesis, age, smoking, and female sex
Hyperlipidemia, hypertension, cigarette smoking, and diabetes mellitus
Male sex, metabolic syndrome, family history, and lipoprotein(a)

answer.reason: The core major modifiable risk factors are hyperlipidemia, hypertension, smoking, and diabetes; inflammation is also highlighted as important.

questions.question:
A 68-year-old smoker is found to have an asymptomatic 5.5 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm.

What is the approximate annual risk of rupture?

answer.reason: AAAs measuring 5 to 6 cm carry an annual rupture risk of about 11%, which is why repair is generally considered at this size range.

questions.question: Where do most abdominal aortic aneurysms typically occur?

choices.text: Between the renal arteries and the aortic bifurcation

answer.reason: Most AAAs arise in the abdominal aorta between the renal arteries and the aortic bifurcation.

questions.question: Which condition is particularly associated with thoracic aortic aneurysm rather than the usual atherosclerotic abdominal aneurysm pattern?

answer.reason: Thoracic aortic aneurysms are commonly associated with hypertension, bicuspid aortic valves, and Marfan syndrome rather than the classic abdominal atherosclerotic pattern.

questions.question: Which description BEST defines aortic dissection?

choices.text: A focal outpouching involving all three layers of the aortic wall
Blood splitting the laminar planes of the media to create a channel within the wall
A mural thrombus adherent to an intact intima
A postinflammatory fibrous scar of the adventitia

answer.reason: Aortic dissection occurs when blood enters the media and splays apart its laminar planes, creating a false channel within the wall.

questions.question:
A 52-year-old man with long-standing hypertension develops sudden excruciating tearing chest pain radiating to the back.

What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

answer.reason: The classic symptom of aortic dissection is sudden tearing or stabbing chest pain radiating to the back, especially in hypertensive middle-aged men.

questions.question:
A 72-year-old woman presents with new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw pain, and transient visual symptoms.

What feared complication makes urgent treatment essential?

answer.reason: Giant cell arteritis can involve the ophthalmic artery and cause sudden permanent blindness, so prompt diagnosis and treatment are critical.

questions.question:
A temporal artery biopsy is negative in a patient whose clinical picture strongly suggests giant cell arteritis.

What is the BEST explanation?

choices.text: The disease never affects the temporal artery
The vascular inflammation is characteristically patchy
Biopsy becomes positive only after steroid therapy

answer.reason: The lesions of giant cell arteritis are notoriously patchy, so a negative temporal artery biopsy does not exclude the diagnosis.

questions.question:
A 28-year-old woman has constitutional symptoms, reduced upper-extremity pulses, visual disturbance, and marked narrowing of the aortic arch branches.

Which vasculitis is MOST likely?

answer.reason: Takayasu arteritis is a granulomatous large-vessel vasculitis of the aortic arch and great vessels that causes pulseless disease and ocular symptoms in younger patients.

questions.question:
A systemic necrotizing vasculitis involves renal and visceral arteries, causes hypertension and abdominal pain, and spares the pulmonary circulation.

Which disorder is MOST likely?

answer.reason: Polyarteritis nodosa is a small- to medium-sized muscular artery vasculitis that classically involves renal and visceral vessels while sparing the lungs.

questions.question:
A 3-year-old child has fever, conjunctival and oral erythema, rash, edema of the hands and feet, and cervical lymphadenopathy.

Why is this disease especially important to recognize?

choices.text: It produces coronary arteritis with aneurysm and thrombosis risk
It causes pulmonary artery dissection in most cases
It is the most common cause of abdominal aortic aneurysm
It is always associated with chronic hepatitis B infection

answer.reason: Kawasaki disease is clinically important because coronary arteritis can produce aneurysm, thrombosis, and myocardial infarction.

questions.question: Which ANCA specificity is most strongly associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis?

answer.reason: PR3-ANCA is the classic ANCA associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis.

questions.question:
A patient has chronic sinus disease, lung nodules, and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

Which diagnosis BEST fits this triad?

answer.reason: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis classically combines upper/lower respiratory tract granulomatous disease with necrotizing glomerulonephritis.

questions.question:
A healthy young woman develops episodic white-blue-red color changes of the fingers during cold exposure, without evidence of systemic autoimmune disease.

What is the MOST likely mechanism?

choices.text: Exaggerated vasomotor responses to cold or emotion

answer.reason: Primary Raynaud phenomenon is caused by exaggerated central and local vasomotor responses, typically without early structural vessel disease.

questions.question:
A woman with long-standing lower-limb varicosities develops chronic edema, pain, stasis dermatitis, and a poorly healing ulcer near the ankle.

What pathophysiologic change underlies these complications?

answer.reason: Varicose dilation renders venous valves incompetent, producing venous stasis, edema, pain, skin change, and chronic ulceration.

questions.question: In deep venous thrombosis of the legs, which factor is considered the MOST important risk factor?

answer.reason: Prolonged immobilization is the most important risk factor for lower-extremity DVT because it promotes venous stasis.

questions.question:
A woman develops chronic arm swelling and peau d'orange change after axillary lymph-node dissection for breast carcinoma.

What is the MOST likely explanation?

choices.text: Secondary obstructive lymphedema from severed lymphatic connections

answer.reason: Secondary lymphedema commonly follows tumor treatment that disrupts previously normal lymphatic drainage, leading to chronic edema and fibrotic skin change.
